data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Mass movements of displaced maritime communities formed autonomous seafaring coalitions that, seeking new trade routes, systematically disrupted inter‑city maritime commerce between 1200‑1150 BCE.","mechanism": "Network fragmentation reduced exchange of essential commodities (e.g., tin, oil), causing economic contraction and political destabilization in affected polities.","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece","Ugarit","Levantine city‑states"],"excludes": ["Hittite Anatolia","Egypt"]},"forbidden": [{"case": "Contemporary Linear B tablets record uninterrupted grain imports from the Aegean during the crisis period", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}],"prose_notes": "Focuses on commercial network disruption rather than violent raids, differentiating it from the canonical Sea Peoples narrative."}
